sentences.text: My name is Ben Roter.
R-O-T-E-R.
1915, In January.
In Pulawy.
[NON-ENGLISH] Lubelskie.
Poland.
80 Years. 80 and a half years.
Well, my family was a big family.
I had four sisters.
I had four sisters, a father, a mother.
And we made a good living there.
We was-- My father was a saddlemaker and an upholsterer.
When I was 15 years old, 14, 15 years old, I started to learn the trade.
And I was working with my father, with my family.
All together in the family.
I went to school in the [NON-ENGLISH] school.
I finished school seven grades, like in Poland.
And then I start to work in the family.
Religious?
Well, my father, my family was not too religious.
But they were religious people.
I used to belong to an organization, [?
Gudonya. ?] Every night we used to go out and spend time in the organization.
We had a good time.
Lots of friends in the organization.
Oh, we used to go to Yom Kippur.
Every Shabbos, every Saturday we used to go with my father to the synagogue.
And 12 o'clock we came home, eat dinner like Shabbat, you know.
We had a good time.
It was a nice family, a good family.
I miss them.
Pulawy?
Well--
Well, over there was in Pulawy was 7,000 Jewish families, Jewish families.
That was a nice organizations.
All kind of organizations, but I used to belong to [?
Gudonya. ?] We used to have a good time.
Saturdays and Sundays we used to go out to the Vistula, to the Wisla.
We used to-- in summertime we used to go on the plaza there.
We used to spend good times.
We used to have with my friends good times.
For fun?
Well, I used to spend with my friends.
Every Saturday night we used to have a dance.
We used to go to the dance-- we used to-- together.
Even with the Polish people too.
Even with the Polish people.
We spent good times.
We had a good time.
We had good, good, good years at that time.
Yeah.
Well, in 1938 we start to talk about Hitler and this and that, you know.
But we didn't know that they're going to do what they did.
We didn't know.
Nobody knows.
In 1939 they occupied Pulawy, Pulawy.
And as soon as they occupied Pulawy, right away, in three or four days they make a ghetto.
And I had my brother-in-law, he was in the army, in the Polish army.
And he said, Benny, we have to run out, because they are going to-- they're going to make ghettos.
We have to run.
We are young.
I will still 23, 24 years old.
And he organized a group for 10 or 12 boys in Pulawy.
In the nighttime we went.
We escaped from Pulawy to Lublin.
And from Lublin we went to Wlodawa.
And next was the Bug.
And we went to the Bug, and we went to Brest-Litovsk.
But the last day, the last-- the moment when I left Pulawy, my father make me a rucksack.
And he said to me-- [CRYING] he said to me, leave us, son.
God be with you.
That God bless you.
And that's the last time I saw my father and mother.
From there, we're on to Lublin.
Again, the same thing.
We went to Lublin.
We went to Bug.
And then we went on the Russian side.
We were safe on the Russian side, because the Russians, they didn't kill Jewish people.
But they killed-- Germans killed.
And I lined up in Brest-Litovsk.
Then they took me to work.
The Russians, they took we to work.
They took us about three or four weeks.
We went to the Russian city.
They called it Surazh.
I was working there in a factory from paper, a paper factory.
And we were working there very hard.
I used to-- I used to work in a-- I used to work in a factory, and we used to [INAUDIBLE] turf, like turf.
It was very hard for us. 23 years, a boy, what we know about so hard work?
And then I decided I will run back to the borders of like Kovel.
We ran back.
We run back.
We was there-- we was living in Kovel.
In Kovel was-- This is on the Polish side?
This is-- that was on the German-- on the Russian side.
But that wasn't, that wasn't 99.
That was supposed to be 90, 100 kilometer from the border.
From the Bug.
The Bug is the River Bug.
The Bug is the border between Russians and Germany.
They divided-- Stalin and Hitler divided that.
And I couldn't be in Kovel because that wasn't 100 kilometers.
So we have to run.
We left Kovel to Krzemieniec, Krzemieniec [NON-ENGLISH].
Over there was a town.
The whole town was in the hills.
Beautiful town.
I was there through 1941.
And when the Hitler's came in, Germany came in, occupied Krzemieniec.
It took them about three, four days, or five days.
I can't remember how many days.
And they make right away a ghetto.
But lucky, I was an upholsterer and I was a saddlemaker.
They need me to work for them to repair the-- the harnesses-- the harnesses.
And they took me out from that ghetto, and they put me on a farm.
They called this Tetylkowce.
This is about 20 kilometer south of Krzemieniec.
And I was working there.
They feed me.
And they were supposed to give me, every-- every week they supposed to give me a bread, a whole bread.
And all the people that was working that farm, we used to eat together in a restaurant.
This is not a restaurant, but this is a-- they-- they cooked for us, for the workers.
So everything is left from them, I ate it.
And the whole bread, what she gave me for the whole week, that-- piece-- that bread is supposed to last, this is supposed to last for a week.
And that bread, every Sunday, I went from Tetylkowce to that ghetto, 2 o'clock in the morning.
And I took that bread, whatever I could, a few onions, a few whatever and I went.
I had over there a sister and a little sister.
The younger sister.
The oldest sister, and the youngest sister.
I went there at 2 o'clock, 3 o'clock in the morning.
And I went to the ghetto from the side.
And I give it to them, and they divide it between them.
But that was about three months later, they closed the whole ghetto in Krzemieniec.
And I didn't know.
I was on the farm, at the farm.
And they closed the ghetto.
I didn't know that.
And they took all the people from the ghetto and they got rid of them.
Some of them they-- they shoveled them in-- and over there is as shifts from-- from coal.
They put them all in the coals, all the Jewish people.
That's the end of Krzemieniec.
I was in Tetylkowce in that farm.
A Polish boy came over in the morning.
I say, [NON-ENGLISH], you-- you know what happened in Krzemieniec?
I say, no, what happened?
They took all the Jewish people from the ghetto and they-- they killed them.
They killed them all.
They killed them.
I said, what can I do?
Be sure they're going to take you from here, because in that farm.
I came out and I started to think what I'm going to do, where I'm going to run.
I came out from that little house where I was working.
Two guys, two German guys, they came with the rifles.
They say you're arrested.
I say, for what?
I pretend that I didn't know what Krzemieniec.
But the Polack told me that.
He said you're going to Katerburg.
Katerburg was about five kilometer from Tetylkowce.
Krzemieniec was north of Tetylkowce.
Katerburg was south of Tetylkowce, five kilometer.
And they arrest me there.
And they put me in a house, maybe that was an office from the-- from the Ukrainian people, or the Germans.
I didn't know.
I was sitting there about three hours, three hours.
And then they took in another guy, another Jew.
They find him somewhere in the woods or somewhere.
We were both together.
We were both together, and all around was surrounded with the police, Ukrainish police.
The next day I was sleeping there.
The next day I saw lots of people working.
That was not far from that house.
That was from Katerburg over there was a city, Vyshnivets.
They told them they're going to go to Vyshnivets to work.
They have to leave Katerburg.
So the Jewish people, that was maybe 500 or 600 Jewish people, families.
They didn't know where they going.
So they went on the walk.
And I was looking out from the window, far, far away, maybe a kilometer from that house where I was living there.
And I saw old people, young people, with wagons.
Everything was packed on the wagons with the horses.
And I looked out and I said, where are they going?
Where are they going?
The policeman, the Ukrainian that was around me-- he said-- he said they're going-- instead to live in Katerburg, they're going to live in Vyshnivets, in the city Vyshnivets.
But that wasn't it.
A little bit further down, I believe, and I saw it.
That was ditches on the road, next to the road.
I looked far, far away.
And I said, what can be there?
What the ditches?
Later on I find out they [INAUDIBLE]-- they told the Jewish people we have to ditches because the airplanes from the Russians will come.
We have to make deeper the ditches.
They gave them shovels.
All the men, they went into the ditches.
They dig deeper and deeper.
And then two trucks came, armored trucks, German trucks.
The back, and they shoot them out.
They shoot them out.
All the people, they was falling.
From the far I saw just like-- and the kids.
And I see these little kids sitting.
Three months, two months.
And I see these little kids.
They're crying.
They came and they shoveled them in.
It's hard to believe, hard to believe.
Broke my heart, how people can be to people like that.
Hard to believe.
That's the reason I'm crying.
I can speak no more.
So in that time-- so that was a day later.
That was the field where they killed them.
And I tried to find out how I can run away from that house with the other guy.
The other guy was a Jewish fellow too, older man than me.
How we can dig under this, I couldn't find it.
I didn't have a chance to go.
I didn't have a chance to go.
So we was there.
Next day they came and they put us on a wagon with horses.
Who is they?
The Ukrainians?
Germans.
Two Germans.
Not Ukrainian.
Germans.
They came, they put it, and they told the Ukrainian police.
At that time, in that area, Ukrainian was policeman for the Germans.
And they said to take me to Krzemieniec because they brought me from Krzemieniec to Tetylkowce.
They couldn't kill me Katerburg.
Katerburg couldn't kill me.
Krzemieniec.
They took me to Krzemieniec and over there was-- when I passed by Krzemieniec on the street-- I mean, when-- I was on the wagon, you know, with the other guy.
In Krzemieniec was a [NON-ENGLISH], a big [NON-ENGLISH], Krzemieniec [NON-ENGLISH].
They took me to the Krzemieniec [NON-ENGLISH].
Krzemieniec is the city.
Oh, yeah, that's a prison, a jail.
A jail.
[NON-ENGLISH] is a jail.
Oh, I didn't know.
So anyway, they put me in jail.
And the jail was a big fence, big fence about 10 feet high.
And they put me there.
And they put me-- and they put me-- they told me to sit there.
And lots of people, lots of people were sitting.
We wasn't in the jail.
That was-- inside the wall was a-- you know.
A ground.
And everybody was in there.
We shouldn't talk to each other.
We shouldn't talk.
If somebody talk to each other, right away the Ukrainian was sitting on the-- on the-- on the fence, right away they shot him.
So I was sitting and sitting, hungry.
Not a piece of bread.
Dirty.
I'm disappointed.
What I'm going to do?
That's going to be my end.
I'm 24 years old and it's going to be my end.
I was thinking like that.
So from afar was sitting a guy and he said to me-- he didn't say to me.
He winked to me like this.
He said, the guy is watching.
I says in the night time I want you next to me.
His name was Indja Benderski.
He was Krzemieniec, born in Krzemieniec.
Healthy like a bull.
And handsome man and speak Ukrainish like a priest.
When the evening came I rolled over a little bit to him, closer to him, closer to him.
I was sitting next to him.
I say what is my name.
I told him my name.
What is your name?
Where you born?
He said he's in Krzemieniec.
I say what-- what-- what-- what do you want with me?
I say we're running out this night because I have a Ukrainian policeman, he's best my friend.
He's like my brother.
He's a policeman.
He sits at the top of the fence.
I say, how we can go out from here, Indja?
His name  was Indja Benderski.
That was about 10 o'clock, or 10 till, something like this.
The guy form the fence, they-- they put in a rifle, and they give him to pull him out.
He said when he pulls me out, you keep-- you hold my feet too, Indja told me.
And we both.
He saw right away, with me he can go on the road.
With me, he can fight.
With me he can-- because I was young and strong and brains, you know?
I said, OK, Indja.
So the time came, he put down the rifle, the Ukrainian, and pulled out.
He pulled him out.
Right away.
I'm next to him, I pulled his leg.
He pulled us, both of us.
He pulled us both of us up, and we are up.
And then we have to go down.
They kissed each other, the Ukrainian, the policeman.
He was a very good friend.
And he knows every little thing from Krzemieniec.
He knows every place, because he was born in Krzemieniec.
I said, where we going?
He say, go with me.
He grabbed me.
He was taller than me, stronger than me, older than me, about three or four years older.
And we start to run in a mountain, like a hill.
A mountain.
He knows-- everything he knows.
He said you go with me.
Don't run away.
Because by himself-- he doesn't want to be by himself.
Sometimes he get crazy by yourself.
But two people, they can talk over here, there, here, there.
Anyway, we run.
Over there is [NON-ENGLISH]-- a hill, a mountain.
A Polish queen used to live in that.
We ran around and there it is.
We was hiding in the bushes in the nighttime.
In the morning we went to orchard, orchard.
The farmer didn't know that we were there.
And that was very bad.
We was hiding and hiding and hiding.
And then he said to me, I have a good friend.
We'll go to him.
We'll go to him.
Maybe he can hide us.
I say, if you feel that he is a good friend of you, we'll go.
So we went to him in the mountains, you know.
He gave us food and this and that, you know.
And then the guy said, I'm afraid to keep you, because there too danger.
So we have to left.
We left him and we went somewhere else.
We were over there with him about three, four days.
In the nighttime, not in the daytime.
So we went on the street nighttime.
We went on the street.
And over there was two women living in a-- in a little house, a little house.
Later on he told me they was-- before  the war, they was two prostitutes.
And they know him.
The girls, they know him.
Indja Benderski know everybody.
Everybody knows him.
I said, where are we going?
He didn't tell me that they was prostitutes.
So he knocked the door.
We went in.
Two older ladies came out.
And they saw each other.
They didn't know what to do with each other.
He was a very handsome man, Indja Benderski.
We went in.
I say, what do you want?
I say, first of all, give me some food.
They gave us a little bread, whatever they had.
And they left us sleep in that house.
But the girls, they didn't have food to feed us.
Probably they went out to the store-- over there used to be [NON-ENGLISH] to buy bread.
Every person has to have.
And the guy who sold the bread, he saw something wrong.
They're buying too much bread.
Probably they have-- probably they have Jewish, there for hiding.
So they find out that the prostitutes are there, the two girls, the two ladies.
They surrounded the whole house in the nighttime.
And in the front we went in.
In the back was an orchard with plums, with pears, with whatever they had.
And that guy was so good in ears, Indja was so good, he hear something, that this house is surrounded.
So he grabbed me.
He grabbed me.
He said, Butcher.
They used to call me Butcher, too.
Butcher, Boruch, you know.
He grabbed me.
We ran out from the-- from the-- from the window, because the back window was on the side for the-- for the orchard.
We ran out.
And nowhere to hide, nowhere to go.
And they went inside probably.
And we was outside already.
Where to go?
Where to go?
Nowhere to go.
Just take a ditch and bury yourself.
So over there was a toilet, a toilet.
You know, on the farm they have a toilets with a little-- and we went in.
I say, I want to go in the toilet.
And he wants to go in too.
And we went in.
Probably they're not going to go look for us in the toilet.
But they were so close.
They were so close.
And right away, he said, Butcher, you know what, we have to take off that-- if you know what the toilet is in there.
That was a piece of [INAUDIBLE], and around was here, they was sitting here.
We took off that [INAUDIBLE] and we went in so far.
It's round.
It's round, and from the side just a little bit.
And we picked up this, and [INAUDIBLE] was staying this side, and Indja was standing on  this side.
And we took this [INAUDIBLE] up, down to the place.
And the Ukrainian came in and they looked.
They opened the door, and they looked.
There's nobody here.
They didn't think that we are inside.
They left and we were staying there about two and a half hours, three hours in that, inside.
We know-- we thought that the Ukrainian, they're looking for us.
They're looking all over.
You can imagine how we came out from the toilet so far.
We came out.
Nobody is there.
It was maybe 2 o'clock in the morning, 2 o'clock, or 1:30.
We went to his friend, to his friend, an older man.
An older man.
He used to ring the bells in the churches.
And we went to him.
He used to live about two kilometer from that place we were there.
And he saw him.
They know him already too, Indja Bidelski.
They saw him, how he looked.
He said where were you?
We told him the whole story.
I said, I'm going to take you under the roof, you know, under the roof.
And he took us on.
He had lots of stuff-- pairs pants and shirts, because they robbed the Jewish people from the ghetto.
So everybody had it.
So they gave me other pair pants, they gave me other this, other this.
And I was sitting there, sitting there about three, four days.
Three, four days we were sitting there, and they give us food.
But sometimes they find out that guy that rings the bell in the churches, he-- he has-- he must have something in his house.
Jewish people, probably Jewish people.
So the police they occupied, they surrounded his house.
It was nighttime.
It was drizzling like a-- like a-- like a--
Like was raining.
And we were sitting under the ceiling there.
And he grabbed me again and we ran out.
And the Ukrainian was looking for us.
He was looking for us.
